sections.h:
HSMU maintains the ACOS Source Management Guidelines containing a comprehensive system user guide and contemporary user-friendly guidelines to achieve Human Source management processes. Users are to refer to and follow these guidelines. If the Guidelines contradict the Manual, then the Manual is the prevailing authority.

HSMU staff are available to assist with ACOS training. Please contact the Central Registrar for further information.
sections.b: HSMU maintains the ACOS Source Management Guidelines containing a comprehensive system user guide and contemporary user-friendly guidelines to achieve Human Source management processes. Users are to refer to and follow these guidelines. If the Guidelines contradict the Manual, then the Manual is the prevailing authority. HSMU staff are available to assist with ACOS training. Please contact the Central Registrar for further information.

sections.h:
Members of the Internal Affairs Unit, Crime Stoppers and employees on secondment to other agencies that have their own Human Source management systems, are not required to register Human Sources under the procedures outlined in this Manual.

HUMAN SOURCE (Source)

A person who for any reward or identifiable motivation, supplies information to a police employee on matters of interest or facilitates an investigative or intelligence strategy and has an expectation that their identity will be protected.

The Central Registrar will determine whether a person is a Source or a witness for the purposes of determining ongoing management utilising this manual and to assist with the issue of disclosure. If a Source is supplying intelligence/information, they will be treated as a Source and managed on ACOS.
sections.b: Members of the Internal Affairs Unit, Crime Stoppers and employees on secondment to other agencies that have their own Human Source management systems, are not required to register Human Sources under the procedures outlined in this Manual. HUMAN SOURCE (Source) A person who for any reward or identifiable motivation, supplies information to a police employee on matters of interest or facilitates an investigative or intelligence strategy and has an expectation that their identity will be protected. The Central Registrar will determine whether a person is a Source or a witness for the purposes of determining ongoing management utilising this manual and to assist with the issue of disclosure. If a Source is supplying intelligence/information, they will be treated as a Source and managed on ACOS.

sections.h:
Should the Source also be determined to be a witness, all matters relevant to an investigation that the witness is material to, must be recorded upon normal investigative practice systems.

Should the investigating officer or handler of the witness, identify information in the statement that should be disseminated, they should sanitise the information and record it on the IDM system. The investigating officer or handler should request that the details of names in sanitised IDM are withheld until full disclosure of the statement has occurred.

Should it be proposed that the witness become involved as a Civilian Participant in an authorised Controlled Operation (not as a witness), or provide ongoing information, then they must be registered on the ACOS system. Any concerns as to a person’s status or potential utilisation in a Controlled Operation should be directed to the Central Registrar at the earliest opportunity. Please refer to the information provided under the heading ‘Management of Human Source’, later in the Manual.

Responsibility for the preparation of Non-Disclosure Orders and/or affidavits for Public Interest Immunity claims, remain with the investigator and/or the Handler under the provision of Section 138 Criminal Procedures Act. The Central Registrar must review all affidavits prepared for non-disclosure orders prior to service. Any concerns should be directed to the Central Registrar at the earliest opportunity.

The investigating officer or handler should request that the details of names in sanitised IDM are withheld until full disclosure of the statement has occurred. Should it be proposed that the witness become involved as a Civilian Participant in an authorised Controlled Operation (not as a witness), or provide ongoing information, then they must be registered on the ACOS system. Any concerns as to a person’s status or potential utilisation in a Controlled Operation should be directed to the Central Registrar at the earliest opportunity. Please refer to the information provided under the heading ‘Management of Human Source’, later in the Manual. Responsibility for the preparation of Non-Disclosure Orders and/or affidavits for Public Interest Immunity claims, remain with the investigator and/or the Handler under the provision of Section 138 Criminal Procedures Act. The Central Registrar must review all affidavits prepared for non-disclosure orders prior to service. Any concerns should be directed to the Central Registrar at the earliest opportunity.

sections.h:
INSPECTOR – CRIME SUPPORT & SERVICES DIVISION

• Oversight of Central Registrar and their responsibilities, (uses the Central Registrar

role within ACOS) to ensure the safety and security of Sources and WA Police Force employees.

Independent of District / Divisional Inspectors, conducts administrative and “Face to Face” audits of Tier 3 Source.

• Review all Tier 3 Source Registrations and approve or reject.

• Oversees the Central Registrar in authorising reward payments between $3001 and

up to and including $10000 in accordance with the Manual.

• Provide authority for a Source to participate as a civilian in a Controlled Operation.

• May, if considered necessary, authorise a limited delay in rotating a Handler or Co-

Handler.

• Seeks approval from the Commander - State Crime should a deviation from this

manual be considered necessary.
sections.b: INSPECTOR – CRIME SUPPORT & SERVICES DIVISION • Oversight of Central Registrar and their responsibilities, (uses the Central Registrar role within ACOS) to ensure the safety and security of Sources and WA Police Force employees. Independent of District / Divisional Inspectors, conducts administrative and “Face to Face” audits of Tier 3 Source. • Review all Tier 3 Source Registrations and approve or reject. • Oversees the Central Registrar in authorising reward payments between $3001 and up to and including $10000 in accordance with the Manual. • Provide authority for a Source to participate as a civilian in a Controlled Operation. • May, if considered necessary, authorise a limited delay in rotating a Handler or Co- Handler. • Seeks approval from the Commander - State Crime should a deviation from this manual be considered necessary.

sections.h:
• Continually review ACOS to ensure the safety and security of Sources and WA

Police Force employees and compliance with the Manual.

• Continually brief the Inspector, Crime Support & Services Division on Source related

matters especially issues relating to the five key areas of risk being the Handler / Supervisor, the Source, the Agency, the Public, and the Information.

• Review the adequacy of the risk assessment and the risk management proposals

pertaining to use of a Source.

• Ensure the Registration processes are adhered to and records reflect the process

undertaken.

• Considers the approval or rejection of every registration.

• Ensure all Source activities and management of Source comply with the Manual.

• Oversee the integrity of the submission, preparation, handling and ‘safe hands’ delivery

of all 'Letters of Recognition’.

• Oversee and authorise reward payments up to and including $3000 in accordance with

the Manual.

• With Inspector CS&SD approval, oversee reward payments between $3001 and up to

and including $10000 in accordance with the Manual.

Administer the REAC in accordance with the Manual.

• Oversee the recording of discretionary matters relating to alleged offences committed

by a Source.

• Where a Handler has had the day-to-day responsibility for a Tier 3 Source for six

months, provide advice to the Inspector, Crime Support & Services Division that:

o the Handler should be considered for psychological assessment, dependent on

priority and availability;

o the handler is due to be changed; and

o ensure renewal requirements are satisfied by a District / Divisional Inspector,

Supervisor and Handler.

• May, in writing (email), delegate his functions to members of Human Source

Management Unit, whilst maintaining responsibility for those functions.
sections.b: • Continually review ACOS to ensure the safety and security of Sources and WA Police Force employees and compliance with the Manual. • Continually brief the Inspector, Crime Support & Services Division on Source related matters especially issues relating to the five key areas of risk being the Handler / Supervisor, the Source, the Agency, the Public, and the Information. • Review the adequacy of the risk assessment and the risk management proposals pertaining to use of a Source. • Ensure the Registration processes are adhered to and records reflect the process undertaken. • Considers the approval or rejection of every registration. • Ensure all Source activities and management of Source comply with the Manual. • Oversee the integrity of the submission, preparation, handling and ‘safe hands’ delivery of all 'Letters of Recognition’. • Oversee and authorise reward payments up to and including $3000 in accordance with the Manual. • With Inspector CS&SD approval, oversee reward payments between $3001 and up to and including $10000 in accordance with the Manual. Administer the REAC in accordance with the Manual. • Oversee the recording of discretionary matters relating to alleged offences committed by a Source. • Where a Handler has had the day-to-day responsibility for a Tier 3 Source for six months, provide advice to the Inspector, Crime Support & Services Division that: o the Handler should be considered for psychological assessment, dependent on priority and availability; o the handler is due to be changed; and o ensure renewal requirements are satisfied by a District / Divisional Inspector, Supervisor and Handler. • May, in writing (email), delegate his functions to members of Human Source Management Unit, whilst maintaining responsibility for those functions.

sections.h:
• Has administrative oversight over District / Divisional Source and uses the Inspector /

Manager role within ACOS.

• Conduct regular administrative Source audits to ensure Manual and risk requirements

(real or perceived) are satisfied. The audits should evaluate if the Source is still considered to be of value.

• Policy Log all Source administrative audits on ACOS that fall within their area of

responsibility.

SUPERVISOR

• Will be a Sergeant or Acting Sergeant

• Supervise Handlers to ensure Manual requirements and risk (real or perceived) are

satisfied.

• Unless extenuating circumstances exist, ensure a new Source Handler is appointed

every six months if the Source is Tier 3.

• Ensure Source meetings and payments are audio recorded in accordance with this

Manual.

• Review Contact Sheets and endorse the timely dissemination to the Central Registrar.

• Liaise with other Managers / Supervisors in order to provide cross

Division/District/Region/ Portfolio support to operations.

• Ensure compliance and effective management with payments (including REAC) to

Source. Attend meetings with the Source in company with the Handler and Co-Handler where REAC rewards are to be made in accordance with this Manual.

• Verify payments on ACOS.

• Evaluate information and ensure the dissemination of actionable intelligence only takes

place in a safe form using ACOS.

HANDLER

• Ensure the Source is clear about their role, conditions and personal security and that

they remain at all times under the Handler's control.

• Ensure the Source has a secure means of contact with the Handler and Co-Handler.

• In conjunction with the Supervisor, ensure risk assessments are accurate and

comprehensive.

• Complete a ‘Meeting Request Form’ for any meeting or contact to be conducted with

a Source.

• Promptly report all meetings or contact with Source to the HSM / Supervisor.

• Attend all ‘In Person Meetings’ with Source with an approved Co-Handler.

• Ensure Source fully understand their terms and conditions (Appendix A) and signs it.

Where the Source does not sign the document, their registration is not to be progressed.

• Ensure appropriate endeavours are undertaken to protect the identity of a Source

whilst being aware that in certain circumstances, the court may exercise discretion and require the identity of the Source to be revealed.

• Submit rewards and working expenses in relation to the management of Source.

• Where an Intelligence Analyst is to participate in a Source meeting and conditions

relative to contact with the Source have been stipulated, ensure the analyst is present when the handler presents the Operational Security Plan Briefing.

CO-HANDLER

• Provides support to the Handler in the management of a Source.

• The role of a Co-Handler is to provide support and assist the Handler in managing the

source. A Co-Handler is NOT to assume the role of the handler unless they hold the appropriate qualifications.

sections.h: Applies to all WA Police Force employees. The Management of Health and Safety at Work Regulations 1992 require that all significant risks be identified. For Health and Safety Legislation, Source are not 'employees' if used in accordance with the procedures contained in this Manual. The risk assessment procedures referenced in the Manual help manage the risks to Source and others.

The risk assessment procedures referenced in the Manual help manage the risks to Source and others.

sections.h:
A registration is created in ACOS by the Handler who completes a Source Profile, Authorisation, and a Risk Assessment. A Registration becomes active once it is authorised by the Central Registrar.

REVIEWS

ACOS will generate a “review” every ninety days. If satisfied that the criteria necessary for the initial authorisation continues to be met, the Handler will complete the review document and forward it to the Supervisor of the Source with a recommendation to Continue / Discontinue.

CANCELLATIONS

The Central Registrar, in consultation with the Handler or Supervisor must cancel an authorisation:

• if the Source no longer satisfies the criteria for authorisation; or

• management of the Source is not compliant with the Manual; or

• the level of risk has increased and may no longer be managed or

• the intelligence provided by the Source is not reliable.

The Source must be informed (where operationally practicable) that the authorisation has been cancelled.

The cancellation should provide a brief evaluation of the Source’s value, credibility and risk associated with the registration.
sections.b: A registration is created in ACOS by the Handler who completes a Source Profile, Authorisation, and a Risk Assessment. A Registration becomes active once it is authorised by the Central Registrar. REVIEWS ACOS will generate a “review” every ninety days. If satisfied that the criteria necessary for the initial authorisation continues to be met, the Handler will complete the review document and forward it to the Supervisor of the Source with a recommendation to Continue / Discontinue. CANCELLATIONS The Central Registrar, in consultation with the Handler or Supervisor must cancel an authorisation: • if the Source no longer satisfies the criteria for authorisation; or • management of the Source is not compliant with the Manual; or • the level of risk has increased and may no longer be managed or • the intelligence provided by the Source is not reliable. The Source must be informed (where operationally practicable) that the authorisation has been cancelled. The cancellation should provide a brief evaluation of the Source’s value, credibility and risk associated with the registration.

sections.h:
Provision is made in ACOS to record opportunistic one-off information capture events.

The process allows the Supervisor to authorise the Authorisation form and make the Registration Active without the Central Registrar being involved. These Registrations become inactive after 5 days without a cancellation being required.

When registering a human source as a one off capture, HSMU on call is to be contacted who will make a suitability assessment of the Human Source.

HIGH RISK HUMAN SOURCE – CONTROL MEASURES

If an officer believes a Source to be high risk at any time whilst Authorised, consultation must be made with the Central Registrar who will assist to determine the most effective control measures for mitigation.

Examples of control measures include;

• Source exposure risk; Handlers are to ensure all current and future investigative

strategies consider the risk that they may pose to the Source and ensure perpetual risk mitigation is applied.

• Risk management; The Handler is to perpetually assess risks relating to the Source.

Where it has identified the current risk rating has, or is highly likely to escalate, the Central Registrar is to be informed immediately.

• Qualifications; Handler and Supervisor to have requisite credentials – Level 3 trained

and Negative Vetting 1 with a Source participating in a Controlled Operation.

sections.h:
The decision to defer charging a Source with an offence committed by them is made and managed by the relevant District or Divisional Superintendent where the investigating officer is attached. A source with deferred charges is to be considered High Risk.

DISCRETION

The exercise of discretion not to charge a Source with an offence committed by them is a matter for consideration by a Commissioned Officer from the District or Division responsible for the handling of the Source.

Whether or not to charge a Source with an offence must be considered in line with the DPP Prosecution Guidelines and related WA Police Force guidelines and policies.

WA Police Force requirements relevant to the exercise of discretion not to charge can be found in:

PP-01.01 Statement of Prosecuting Policy and Principles PP-01.02 Exercise of Discretionary Power Guidelines

PP-01.05 Prosecution of Police Human Source and Court Based Benefits
sections.b: The decision to defer charging a Source with an offence committed by them is made and managed by the relevant District or Divisional Superintendent where the investigating officer is attached. A source with deferred charges is to be considered High Risk. DISCRETION The exercise of discretion not to charge a Source with an offence committed by them is a matter for consideration by a Commissioned Officer from the District or Division responsible for the handling of the Source. Whether or not to charge a Source with an offence must be considered in line with the DPP Prosecution Guidelines and related WA Police Force guidelines and policies. WA Police Force requirements relevant to the exercise of discretion not to charge can be found in: PP-01.01 Statement of Prosecuting Policy and Principles PP-01.02 Exercise of Discretionary Power Guidelines PP-01.05 Prosecution of Police Human Source and Court Based Benefits

sections.h:
The Director of Public Prosecutions is the only person with statutory authority to grant an indemnity from prosecution. (Refer to sub-sections 20(2)(c) and 20(2)(d) of the Director of Public Prosecutions Act 1991 and paragraph 42 onwards of the DPP Statement of Prosecution Policy and Guidelines 2022.)

If it is identified during an investigation that a Source is likely to be of more value as a prosecution witness than an accused, and potentially worthy of an indemnity under the Act, the WA Police Force should seek advice from the Director before proceeding on that basis.
sections.b: The Director of Public Prosecutions is the only person with statutory authority to grant an indemnity from prosecution. (Refer to sub-sections 20(2)(c) and 20(2)(d) of the Director of Public Prosecutions Act 1991 and paragraph 42 onwards of the DPP Statement of Prosecution Policy and Guidelines 2022.) If it is identified during an investigation that a Source is likely to be of more value as a prosecution witness than an accused, and potentially worthy of an indemnity under the Act, the WA Police Force should seek advice from the Director before proceeding on that basis.

sections.h:
TASKING

Tasking is the deployment or assignment given to the Source by those involved in the management of the Source to obtain information or intelligence to achieve an investigative or operational outcome. Authorisation for the Use or Conduct of a Source is to be obtained prior to any tasking where such tasking requires the Source to establish or maintain a personal or other relationship for a covert purpose.

Tasking must have clearly defined objectives and demonstrate the Source’s ability to achieve objectives aligned with agency / business area priorities or investigative objectives. Sources must be made aware of and acknowledge that;

• They may be asked to perform tasks for the WA Police Force and if they agree to

perform the tasks, they must complete them as instructed by a Handler or Co-handler;

and,

• That other than in accordance with an authority issued under the Criminal Investigation

(Covert Powers) Act 2012, they are not permitted to commit any unlawful act or to incite, encourage or assist another person to commit any unlawful act.

Any concern that a Source may have committed an offence or, it is intended that the Source is to be used in a controlled operation, must be communicated to the Central Registrar at the earliest opportunity.
sections.b: TASKING Tasking is the deployment or assignment given to the Source by those involved in the management of the Source to obtain information or intelligence to achieve an investigative or operational outcome. Authorisation for the Use or Conduct of a Source is to be obtained prior to any tasking where such tasking requires the Source to establish or maintain a personal or other relationship for a covert purpose. Tasking must have clearly defined objectives and demonstrate the Source’s ability to achieve objectives aligned with agency / business area priorities or investigative objectives. Sources must be made aware of and acknowledge that; • They may be asked to perform tasks for the WA Police Force and if they agree to perform the tasks, they must complete them as instructed by a Handler or Co-handler; and, • That other than in accordance with an authority issued under the Criminal Investigation (Covert Powers) Act 2012, they are not permitted to commit any unlawful act or to incite, encourage or assist another person to commit any unlawful act. Any concern that a Source may have committed an offence or, it is intended that the Source is to be used in a controlled operation, must be communicated to the Central Registrar at the earliest opportunity.

sections.h:
A human source may be tasked to undertake activity which will require protection from criminal responsibility. This protection is authorised in a Controlled Operation by the Delegate of the Commissioner of Police under a Criminal Investigation (Covert Powers) Act 2012. The authorised activity the human source undertakes must be at the instruction of the Handlers/Supervisors. If the human source acts outside of any instruction given by the Handlers/Supervisors, they risk the protection from criminal responsibility afforded to them under a Controlled Operation.

Controlled Operations involving a Source must be recorded as a “Participation” and be approved by the Inspector, Crime Support & Services Division prior to the Controlled Operations application being progressed to the Authorising Officer. The conduct and additional Risk Assessment details specific to that conduct must be addressed and reflect deployment and risk mitigation strategies formulated for the Source.
sections.b: A human source may be tasked to undertake activity which will require protection from criminal responsibility. This protection is authorised in a Controlled Operation by the Delegate of the Commissioner of Police under a Criminal Investigation (Covert Powers) Act 2012. The authorised activity the human source undertakes must be at the instruction of the Handlers/Supervisors. If the human source acts outside of any instruction given by the Handlers/Supervisors, they risk the protection from criminal responsibility afforded to them under a Controlled Operation. Controlled Operations involving a Source must be recorded as a “Participation” and be approved by the Inspector, Crime Support & Services Division prior to the Controlled Operations application being progressed to the Authorising Officer. The conduct and additional Risk Assessment details specific to that conduct must be addressed and reflect deployment and risk mitigation strategies formulated for the Source.

sections.h:
When a Source is tasked as a civilian participant, the Source is given authority to engage in particular ‘controlled conduct’, at the direction of a ‘Handler’, who must hold Human Source Level 3 qualification.

A Source can be authorised to participate in an operation as a civilian participant on a confidential basis, which means that the assistance provided by the Source is protected to the greatest extent practicable.

Otherwise, a Source who is tasked as a civilian participant does so as a potential prosecution witness.

The distinction between a Source being tasked as a civilian participant in a confidential capacity or as a potential witness, is important when considering disclosure obligations under the Criminal Procedure Act 2004.
sections.b: When a Source is tasked as a civilian participant, the Source is given authority to engage in particular ‘controlled conduct’, at the direction of a ‘Handler’, who must hold Human Source Level 3 qualification. A Source can be authorised to participate in an operation as a civilian participant on a confidential basis, which means that the assistance provided by the Source is protected to the greatest extent practicable. Otherwise, a Source who is tasked as a civilian participant does so as a potential prosecution witness. The distinction between a Source being tasked as a civilian participant in a confidential capacity or as a potential witness, is important when considering disclosure obligations under the Criminal Procedure Act 2004.

sections.h:
PARTICIPANT

(1) The Handler is required to complete a ‘Participation’ within ACOS and complete the

‘New Risks Identified’ section within the participation that is particular to the controlled conduct the Source is to engage in. The Central Registrar is to review and authorise the participation.

(2) The tasking of a civilian participant is considered high risk and must be undertaken by

a ‘Handler’ who has completed Human Source Management Training, Level 3.

(3) Tasking must be recorded by the Handler on a Tasking Request form in ACOS.

(4) A Commissioned Officer from the Handler’s Division/District must provide approval of

the proposed operational strategy.

(5) The Central Registrar must provide the Inspector, Crime Support & Services Division

with recommendations about the proposed use of the Source as a civilian participant.

(6) The Inspector, Crime Support & Services Division is required to consider suitability of

the Source to engage in the proposed controlled conduct and may approve use, or not.

The Central Registrar should be contacted for advice at the earliest opportunity if it is being considered that a Source is to be authorised as a civilian participant.
sections.b: PARTICIPANT (1) The Handler is required to complete a ‘Participation’ within ACOS and complete the ‘New Risks Identified’ section within the participation that is particular to the controlled conduct the Source is to engage in. The Central Registrar is to review and authorise the participation. (2) The tasking of a civilian participant is considered high risk and must be undertaken by a ‘Handler’ who has completed Human Source Management Training, Level 3. (3) Tasking must be recorded by the Handler on a Tasking Request form in ACOS. (4) A Commissioned Officer from the Handler’s Division/District must provide approval of the proposed operational strategy. (5) The Central Registrar must provide the Inspector, Crime Support & Services Division with recommendations about the proposed use of the Source as a civilian participant. (6) The Inspector, Crime Support & Services Division is required to consider suitability of the Source to engage in the proposed controlled conduct and may approve use, or not. The Central Registrar should be contacted for advice at the earliest opportunity if it is being considered that a Source is to be authorised as a civilian participant.

sections.h:
The Source Registration process requires that a Handler complete a risk assessment on ACOS. The risk assessment contains pre-loaded risks and control measures to assist in the process.

The risk assessment is authorised by the Supervisor before progressing to the Central Registrar for review and approval or rejection.

Risk assessments are also required during the Source renewal process and when it is proposed that a Source participate in a controlled operation.
sections.b: The Source Registration process requires that a Handler complete a risk assessment on ACOS. The risk assessment contains pre-loaded risks and control measures to assist in the process. The risk assessment is authorised by the Supervisor before progressing to the Central Registrar for review and approval or rejection. Risk assessments are also required during the Source renewal process and when it is proposed that a Source participate in a controlled operation.

sections.h:
(ARD)

The use of an ARD should not be disclosed to the Source and the audio recording must not be saved to any other device or hard drive unless using OneDrive for the purposes of uploading content off a OneForce phone. ARDs utilised must have the ability to be pin locked.

ARDs should only have internal storage and no removable SD Card. ARDs that have the ability to digitally encrypt audio are preferred, however, are not mandated for use.

The following procedures apply to the use of ARDs and recordings:

• All contact with a Source must be audio recorded.

• Any contact involving payment to a Source (regardless of Tier) must be audio

recorded. Handlers are responsible for ensuring ARDs are ready for use, and where operationally possible used for the recording of all meetings and payments.

• The Supervisor is to ensure audio recordings are uploaded to ACOS in a timely

manner.

• Audio file names should conform with the naming convention as provided by the CR

and cross referenced with the relevant Contact Sheet.

• Handlers must delete audio recordings from the ARD once upload has been

completed.

• All audio recordings must comply with Section 5(3) of the Surveillance Devices Act

1989.

• If for some reason the contact isn’t or cannot be recorded, the contact description is to

fully account for the conversation had with the Source and the reason the conversation was not audio recorded.

• There is no requirement to audio record meetings with a Tier 1 (Community Source)

unless making a payment to that person.

Handlers and Supervisors must ensure disclosure obligations are complied with. Where a legislative requirement dictates, the Supervisor should contact the Investigating Officer and ensure that a Non-Disclosure Order is considered / obtained and an appropriate record made on the Sensitive Material List.
sections.b: (ARD) The use of an ARD should not be disclosed to the Source and the audio recording must not be saved to any other device or hard drive unless using OneDrive for the purposes of uploading content off a OneForce phone. ARDs utilised must have the ability to be pin locked. ARDs should only have internal storage and no removable SD Card. ARDs that have the ability to digitally encrypt audio are preferred, however, are not mandated for use. The following procedures apply to the use of ARDs and recordings: • All contact with a Source must be audio recorded. • Any contact involving payment to a Source (regardless of Tier) must be audio recorded. Handlers are responsible for ensuring ARDs are ready for use, and where operationally possible used for the recording of all meetings and payments. • The Supervisor is to ensure audio recordings are uploaded to ACOS in a timely manner. • Audio file names should conform with the naming convention as provided by the CR and cross referenced with the relevant Contact Sheet. • Handlers must delete audio recordings from the ARD once upload has been completed. • All audio recordings must comply with Section 5(3) of the Surveillance Devices Act 1989. • If for some reason the contact isn’t or cannot be recorded, the contact description is to fully account for the conversation had with the Source and the reason the conversation was not audio recorded. • There is no requirement to audio record meetings with a Tier 1 (Community Source) unless making a payment to that person. Handlers and Supervisors must ensure disclosure obligations are complied with. Where a legislative requirement dictates, the Supervisor should contact the Investigating Officer and ensure that a Non-Disclosure Order is considered / obtained and an appropriate record made on the Sensitive Material List.

sections.h:
A Policy Log records information from any user and should include:

• decisions on the use of the Source;

• Supervisor approval to use alternative communications other than voice calls;

• strategy decisions relating to the Source;

• plans for development of the Source;

• discretion/deferrals; and

• reviews and administrative audit outcomes conducted by a District / Divisional

Inspector and the Inspector, Crime Support & Services Division.
sections.b: A Policy Log records information from any user and should include: • decisions on the use of the Source; • Supervisor approval to use alternative communications other than voice calls; • strategy decisions relating to the Source; • plans for development of the Source; • discretion/deferrals; and • reviews and administrative audit outcomes conducted by a District / Divisional Inspector and the Inspector, Crime Support & Services Division.

sections.h:
Communication between the Handler (including unsolicited) and the Source should be restricted to in person meetings or by a voice (telephone) call. If it is necessary to use an alternative form of communication outside of these two options, this may be carried out with prior approval from the Supervisor.

The request to use an alternative communication medium must be documented in the initial authorisation and approved by the Supervisor.

If the decision is made to move to an alternative communication method during the current authorisation period, the Supervisor must justify the reason in the Policy Log.

Upon the 90 – day review, if alternative communications are still required, the decision must be documented in the Review and approved by the Supervisor.

The request to use an alternative communication medium must be documented in the initial authorisation and approved by the Supervisor. If the decision is made to move to an alternative communication method during the current authorisation period, the Supervisor must justify the reason in the Policy Log. Upon the 90 – day review, if alternative communications are still required, the decision must be documented in the Review and approved by the Supervisor.

sections.h:
CORRUPTION

(By employees of law enforcement or government agencies) The Central Registrar will be responsible for ensuring an accountable and transparent process is in place for the accurate and timely dissemination of information that alleges misconduct or corruption (perceived or actual).

Prior to dissemination of information alleging corruption by police officers or public officials, the Inspector, Crime Support & Services Division will be briefed. The information will be processed through HSMU directly to the Internal Affairs Unit or the Corruption and Crime Commission via the IDM system or encrypted email.

Reports that require caveats to be applied in IDM will be managed by the HSMU who will disseminate directly to IAU/CCC.
sections.b: CORRUPTION (By employees of law enforcement or government agencies) The Central Registrar will be responsible for ensuring an accountable and transparent process is in place for the accurate and timely dissemination of information that alleges misconduct or corruption (perceived or actual). Prior to dissemination of information alleging corruption by police officers or public officials, the Inspector, Crime Support & Services Division will be briefed. The information will be processed through HSMU directly to the Internal Affairs Unit or the Corruption and Crime Commission via the IDM system or encrypted email. Reports that require caveats to be applied in IDM will be managed by the HSMU who will disseminate directly to IAU/CCC.

sections.h:
All Rewards and Benefits must be authorised and recorded on ACOS and are classified on ACOS as either:

• single payment;

• staged payment (Multi);

• working expense; or

• letter of recognition.

Considerable risks may arise because of significant one-off payments to Source. The sudden appearance of large sums of money, vouchers or material goods requires a plausible explanation and careful management if the Source is not to run the risk of exposure.
sections.b: All Rewards and Benefits must be authorised and recorded on ACOS and are classified on ACOS as either: • single payment; • staged payment (Multi); • working expense; or • letter of recognition. Considerable risks may arise because of significant one-off payments to Source. The sudden appearance of large sums of money, vouchers or material goods requires a plausible explanation and careful management if the Source is not to run the risk of exposure.

sections.h:
Cash payments for working expenses to Source must be audio recorded.

Handlers and Supervisors will require access to working expenses to meet the running costs of routine Source activity. The provision of monies for working expenses 'in advance' to a Source should be within the scope of the authority requested by Handlers.

In some cases, the ability of a Source to secure and maintain access to the area of reporting on which they have been tasked, may require the payment of 'working expenses'.

All working expenses must be authorised by the Central Registrar prior to payment. Verbal approval may be granted by the Central Registrar.

A working expense is not to be utilised as a pseudo reward. Working expenses may be attributable to tasking and if approved by the Central Registrar, rapport building.

Where practicable, a receipt for the incurred expense is required from the Source.

Audio recorded acknowledgement by a Source of receival of anything other than cash, should be obtained.

Where practicable a supporting record of receival should be obtained.

Examples of working expenses include meeting expenses, communication costs, car rental, sustenance, costs to assist tasking etc. Contact the Central Registrar for further information.
sections.b: Cash payments for working expenses to Source must be audio recorded. Handlers and Supervisors will require access to working expenses to meet the running costs of routine Source activity. The provision of monies for working expenses 'in advance' to a Source should be within the scope of the authority requested by Handlers. In some cases, the ability of a Source to secure and maintain access to the area of reporting on which they have been tasked, may require the payment of 'working expenses'. All working expenses must be authorised by the Central Registrar prior to payment. Verbal approval may be granted by the Central Registrar. A working expense is not to be utilised as a pseudo reward. Working expenses may be attributable to tasking and if approved by the Central Registrar, rapport building. Where practicable, a receipt for the incurred expense is required from the Source. Audio recorded acknowledgement by a Source of receival of anything other than cash, should be obtained. Where practicable a supporting record of receival should be obtained. Examples of working expenses include meeting expenses, communication costs, car rental, sustenance, costs to assist tasking etc. Contact the Central Registrar for further information.

sections.h:
Requests for reward payments are submitted in terms of the monetary value and can be paid to the Source in either single or staged payments. The Central Registrar is responsible for approving Rewards up to and including $3000.

The Central Registrar can approve rewards from $3001 to $10000 after it has been reviewed and approved by the Inspector Crime Support & Services Division. All payments over $10000 require REAC approval.

Reward payments may be made using either of the following methods:

• by cash (signature from Source on receipt);

• by store vouchers or material goods;

• bank transfer into the Source bank account;

• by bank cheque in the Source’s real name; or

• by bank cheque or cash paid directly into the Source’s bank account (account details

must be verified).

The Central Registrar is responsible for ensuring an accountable process is in place for the management of reward payments through the provision of cheques by WA Police Force Finance Section or cash.

All requests for payment of rewards or working expenses must be made to the Central Registrar.

Cash Payments

The payment of cash is conditional upon the Source acknowledging receiving the money.

ACOS generates two receipts for each payment:

• Admin/Finance Receipt – signed by the Handler acknowledging receipt of the money.

• Handler Receipt – must be signed by the Source acknowledging receipt of the money.

The signed receipts are to be scanned and attached to ACOS. The electronic file is to be verified and then the hard copy destroyed.

Store Vouchers and Material Goods

Authorised payments may be converted to Store Vouchers or Material Goods.

The use of this benefit method is encouraged as it assists with rapport building and reduces risks associated with a Source coming into an amount of cash that may not have otherwise been expected by family or associates.

Payment Direct to Source

Where a reward is to be deposited into an account of the Source, the Central Registrar will make the necessary arrangements in a manner which minimises the potential to compromise the Source. Where the Source requires payment other than cash, the Handler should consult with the Central Registrar.

The Supervisor is responsible for verifying these payments.

Petty Cash Float

Crime Support & Services Division - Corporate Services will maintain a cash float for the payment of rewards and working expenses. Appropriate governance and security arrangements will be maintained. The Inspector Crime Support & Services Division will conduct quarterly audits.

The signed receipts are to be scanned and attached to ACOS. The electronic file is to be verified and then the hard copy destroyed. Where the Source requires payment other than cash, the Handler should consult with the Central Registrar. The Supervisor is responsible for verifying these payments. sections.h:
(REAC)

The Committee shall consist of three voting members of the WA Police Force and be comprised of the following;

Permanent Voting Members

• Assistant Commissioner, State Crime or delegate - convene and chair meeting.

• Commander, State Crime.

• Independent Commander.

Permanent Non-voting Members

• Detective Superintendent and/or Inspector, Crime Support & Services Division.

• The respective Supervisor and/or representative of the Command making the

application. (At the discretion of the Assistant Commissioner State Crime).

• Officer in Charge, HSMU or delegate.

The Central Registrar has responsibility for validating the Reward Request and compiling the Reward Pack.
sections.b: (REAC) The Committee shall consist of three voting members of the WA Police Force and be comprised of the following; Permanent Voting Members • Assistant Commissioner, State Crime or delegate - convene and chair meeting. • Commander, State Crime. • Independent Commander. Permanent Non-voting Members • Detective Superintendent and/or Inspector, Crime Support & Services Division. • The respective Supervisor and/or representative of the Command making the application. sections.h:
This section of the Manual relates to an offender who becomes a Source and assists in police investigations on the basis that his or her involvement is kept confidential.

Where a Source assists in police investigations to obtain a sentencing benefit, the sentencing court is required to take that assistance into account.

To enable the court to properly exercise this function, the information which is provided to the court must be adequate to enable the court to understand the assistance that has been provided and to consider the value of that assistance. (MXP v State of WA [2010] WASCA

215; 41 WAR 149)

The mitigatory weight of an offender’s assistance is a matter for the sentencing court to decide. It is the responsibility however of the Prosecutor and the WA Police Force to ensure that the proper information is put before the court to enable the court to undertake an assessment.

The form in which the information is provided to the sentencing court is usually a Letter of Recognition (LOR).

Under arrangements between the Office of the Director of Public Prosecutions (ODPP) and the WA Police Force, the preparation of LORs is the responsibility of the WA Police Force.

CONFIDENTIALITY

A LOR is considered sensitive and confidentiality must be maintained.

The existence of a LOR is to be protected with public interest immunity applying over the content of the document. This means that a LOR is not usually disclosed under the obligations set out in the Criminal Procedure Act 2004.

Subject to a Court order being given, a LOR should not be produced under a witness summons to produce a record or other thing. For further information or assistance relating to the production of LORs under summons, please contact Legal Services Division.

Procedural fairness, however, requires that the Prosecutor and Defence Counsel be aware of the contents of any LOR relied on during a sentencing hearing. This is to ensure that all parties have the opportunity to make submissions on the sentencing considerations (HT v The Queen [2019] HCA 40).

PREPARATION

The Handler is responsible for the LOR request and source results six weeks prior to the Source’s sentencing. The HSMU is responsible for the preparation of a LOR.

Any LOR prepared by the HSMU should contain all the information necessary to enable the sentencing court to make an informed assessment of the weight to be given to assistance provided by the Source.

To obtain the necessary information, the HSMU relies on the Handler to initiate a LOR request within ACOS. The Handler must also arrange for a ‘Lawyers Letter’ to be signed by the Source in which the Source acknowledges that Defence Counsel will be advised of the contents of any LOR that is used in the sentencing process.

Once completed, the HSMU will arrange for the LOR to be provided to the Assistant Commissioner, State Crime for consideration and endorsement.

Should it become necessary to amend the LOR after it has been completed, the HSMU may complete an ‘Addendum’ which is attached to the original letter. The HSMU will notify all relevant parties of any amendment that is made.

sections.h:
District/Supreme Court

HSMU are responsible for:

• delivering a signed LOR to the Director, ODPP at least fourteen days prior to the

sentencing date; and

• providing written confirmation that the offender is aware that, as required by law, the

contents of the LOR will be divulged to Defence Counsel. This is usually done by providing a copy of the ‘Lawyers Letter’.

Upon receipt of these documents, the ODPP will ensure that Defence Counsel are made aware of the contents of the LOR. The ODPP will also arrange to deliver the original LOR to the sentencing court.

Once sentencing has been concluded, the ODPP will return the LOR to HSMU, for secure storage.

Magistrates Court

HSMU are responsible for:

• contacting Defence Counsel and providing information about the content of the LOR.

This is usually done by HSMU reading the content of the letter over the telephone to Defence Counsel;

• contacting the Inspector, Prosecution Services and advising that a LOR has been

prepared for a particular sentencing hearing. The Inspector is required to advise the allocated police prosecutor about the LOR, and the police prosecutor has an obligation to contact HSMU in order to obtain further information about the content of the letter;

and

• delivering a signed LOR to the Chief Magistrate at least four days prior to the

sentencing hearing.

At the conclusion of the sentencing hearing, the Magistrate’s Court will arrange to return the LOR to HSMU for secure storage.

Regional Areas

The following guide is to be followed when serving LORs in Regional Areas.

• Responsibility

Each Inspector in Regional Areas (or his/her delegate) will be responsible for the receipt and delivery of each LOR.

• Process

1. Prepare the LOR Package

▪ Print the LOR Regional Package

▪ Seal the LOR in a plain envelope

▪ Attach the envelope cover sheet to the front of the envelope

2. Serve the LOR

▪ Deliver the sealed envelope to the Magistrates Court Registrar prior to

the trial, plea, or sentence date

▪ Remind court staff that this is a confidential process intended for the

magistrate only

▪ The letter will remain at the court until all matters are finalised

3. Post-Finalisation

▪ Once all matters are finalised, collect the LOR from the court.

▪ Contact the Human Source Management Unit to arrange the

destruction process.

• Confidentiality

1. The documents must NOT be emailed to any person.

2. Access to the LOR is strictly confidential.

• Issues

1. Any issues should be directed to the Central Registrar.

sections.h:
Standard Operating Procedure

Human Source Handler Tactical Options (Accoutrements) requirements for Human Source Meetings

1 Purpose

In accordance with FR-01.02.14 Carriage of Firearms and Tactical Options (Accoutrements) – to authorise the OIC Human Source Management Unit (HSMU) to approve operational members a specific purpose exemption from carrying tactical options (accoutrements) other than a firearm, where it is deemed necessary to do so.

2 Scope

The specific purpose exemption may be granted for members engaged in an approved meeting with a registered or potential Human Source (HS).

3 Specific Procedure

• The OIC HSMU may approve operational members a specific purpose exemption from carrying

tactical options (accoutrements) other than a firearm, where it is deemed necessary to do so.

• The specific purpose exemption may only be granted for officers engaged in an approved meeting

with a registered or potential Human Source.

• The approval will be recorded in writing, in a meeting request on the ACOS system, for the period

required to facilitate the meeting.

• Where an urgent verbal approval is granted a retrospective request will be recorded and highlight

the requirement for the verbal approval.

4 Minimum Standard

• The below table articulates different HS meeting scenarios and minimum tactical options

(accoutrements) required.

Meeting Type V Handlers OPSEC Team Other Risk Mitigation Force Options

Face to Face in ➢ Not Armed ➢ Not Required ➢ Person is in custody Custody ➢ Station Staff to be advised

download ➢ Station Security features restrict movement

Face to Face in ➢ Firearm ➢ Not required ➢ Risk/s considered a Police Station ➢ One other force option ➢ Station Staff to be advised ➢ Station Security features restrict movement

Public Face to ➢ Firearm ➢ Firearm ➢ Risk assessment completed Face pitch ➢ One other force option ➢ One other force option ➢ OPSEC Team on Locate ➢ Cuffs

➢ Vest available

Dead Drop to ➢ Firearm ➢ Firearm ➢ Risk/s considered

or from a ➢ One other force option ➢ One other force option ➢ Action approved by Human Source Manager Source ➢ Cuffs (HSM)

➢ Vest available ➢ No face-to-face contact with Source

➢ OPSEC Team on Locate

Face to Face in ➢ Firearm ➢ Firearm ➢ Risk assessment completed a Vehicle ➢ One other force option ➢ One other force option ➢ Source routed to and from ➢ Cuffs meeting

➢ Vest available ➢ Meeting remotely monitored

Face to Face in ➢ Firearm ➢ Firearm ➢ Risk assessment completed a Hotel ➢ One other force option ➢ One other force option ➢ Source routed to and from ➢ Cuffs meeting

➢ Vest available ➢ Meeting remotely monitored

➢ Have access to the room

Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) Revision 2

5 Responsible Organisation

• OIC HSMU is responsible for managing compliance.

• Crime Support and Services Division ensure staff have access to necessary training and assets.

• HSMU staff are responsible to be currently trained in criticial skills, and to have completed training

for carriage of non-standard equipment (concealeable holster, smaller handguns, concealed secondary force option).

6 Document Approvals

• Detective Senior Sergeant Paul McDonagh, OIC HSMU

• Inspector Darren Taylor, Crime Support and Services Division

• Detective A/Superintendent Brent Fletcher, Crime Support and Services Division

7 Document Administration

7.1 Document Location

Human Source Management Unit P: drive and HSMU Blue Book (reference in Induction pack) 7.2 Document References

Police Manual Entry FR-01.02.14 Carriage of Firearms and Tactical Options (Accoutrements) 7.3 Amendment History

• Created 21 May 2025

• Authorised 1 August 2025

PURPOSE

These procedures have been compiled for members of the Western Australia Police Force (WA Police Force) to ensure uniformity regarding the use of pretext telephone conversations.

The WA Police Force utilise pretext telephone conversations as an investigative tool and it is incumbent upon all officers to be conversant with the procedures and legal requirements associated with pretext telephone conversations.

Any feedback or recommendations pertaining to these procedures should be directed to the Investigative Standards Unit.

These procedures relate to the use of pretext telephone conversations between two parties to capture evidence in relation to an alleged offence.

To ensure all legal requirements are met during the recording of the conversation and to ensure admissibility, officers are advised to adhere to the standard operating procedures as set out in this document.

PURPOSE These procedures have been compiled for members of the Western Australia Police Force (WA Police Force) to ensure uniformity regarding the use of pretext telephone conversations. The WA Police Force utilise pretext telephone conversations as an investigative tool and it is incumbent upon all officers to be conversant with the procedures and legal requirements associated with pretext telephone conversations. Any feedback or recommendations pertaining to these procedures should be directed to the Investigative Standards Unit. These procedures relate to the use of pretext telephone conversations between two parties to capture evidence in relation to an alleged offence. To ensure all legal requirements are met during the recording of the conversation and to ensure admissibility, officers are advised to adhere to the standard operating procedures as set out in this document.

sections.h:
POLICIES

Telecommunications (Interception and Access) Act 1979

Section 5(1)(b), Surveillance Devices Act 1998

Section 5(3)(b), Surveillance Devices Act 1998

Section 13, Surveillance Devices Act 1998

INTRODUCTION

A pretext telephone conversation is an investigative tool which consists of a recorded conversation, for evidentiary purposes, between a suspect, the complainant and or witness involved in the investigation. It is called a pretext conversation because the complainant telephones the suspect on the pretext of discussing the alleged offence under investigation.

There are strict rules governing pretext conversations to ensure integrity of any ensuing evidence content is protected. Great care should be taken to ensure the application of this strategy does not contravene the relevant legislation relating to interception of telephone calls and the use of surveillance devices. It is prudent to corroborate the information provided to investigators by the complainant and any other witnesses. These offences are often committed by a person known to the complainant, but perpetrated behind closed doors where the only persons present are the offender and complainant.

Legal Background and Case Law

Current Commonwealth and State legislation affects the circumstances under which pretext telephone calls can be conducted. Recent case law has also been provided to investigators which will assist in their understanding of this line of investigation.

Authority to Make a Pretext Telephone Call

The interception and recording of communications passing over a telephone service is regulated by Commonwealth legislation (Telecommunications (Interception and Access) Act 1979). Under that Act it is an offence for a person, without the knowledge of a person making a communication, to intercept a communication passing over a telecommunication system.

In order to establish an offence it must be shown that the offender intercepted the communication whilst it was passing over the service.

In the NSW case of Oliver (1984) 57 ALR 543 it was held that once the words of the communication have left the actual phone service (on the facts of the case the words being listened to via a microphone that was external to the telephone) then there is no interception and accordingly no offence committed. A similar conclusion was reached in R v East [2003] NTSC 42 where it was held recording a telephone conversation by use of an ear piece and tape recorder by a party to the conversation does not constitute an interception.

However, an offence may be committed under state legislation where a private conversation is recorded by one party to it without knowledge of the other party (refer Section 5(1)(b) Surveillance Devices Act 1998). An exception to this rule is contained in Section 5(3)(b) of the Act. In essence, no offence is committed by a person acting at the direction of a law enforcement officer who is carrying out an investigation into the commission of a suspected criminal offence. This being the case the recording of a conversation between a suspect and a person (e.g. complainant) in the course of a pretext call under the instruction of a police officer investigating an offence is not unlawful. It should be noted this “protection” does not extend to a person, not a party to the conversation, who listens in. Investigating officers are not authorised to monitor the conversation in “real time” unless a warrant has been obtained under Section 13 of the Act.

Admissibility of Evidence Obtained During a Pretext Call The object of a pretext call is to obtain a confession, or, at the very least, an admission or admissions against interest by the suspect. When material of this nature is obtained the question of admissibility will be determined by (a) the lawfulness of the conduct of the police in obtaining the evidence and (b) whether, in the particular circumstances of the case, the evidence was obtained improperly or in such a way that it would be unfair to admit it into evidence. This will require the court to consider all the facts and exercise discretion to exclude or admit.

Where police have complied with the requirements of the Commonwealth and State legislation cited above the key consideration will be whether or not it would be unfair to the accused to allow the material into evidence. This will require “an examination of all the circumstances which may point to unfairness to the accused if the [material] is admitted”

(Pavitt v The Queen (2007) 169 A Crim R 452). In Pavitt the court identified a number of factors to be taken into account in exercising the discretion to exclude or admit:

• It may be unfair to admit material into evidence where the accused had previously

indicated he/she did not want to speak to police about the matter and the person acting at the request of the police causes the accused to make the admission.

• It may be unfair to admit material into evidence where the person acting at the request

of the police conducted what was effectively an interrogation of the accused.

• It may be unfair to admit material into evidence where the person acting on behalf of

the police took advantage of any special relationship existing between him/her and the accused or exploited any personal vulnerability of him/her.

• Where the conversation if covertly recorded, i.e. without the knowledge of the

accused that of itself will not make admission into evidence unfair (at least where the recording was made lawfully).

• Where the conversation between the accused and the person acting on behalf of the

police does not amount to a form of interrogation, and there is no evidence of any special relationship with the accused being exploited, then it cannot be said there has been a violation of the right to silence. The accused has exercised his/her choice to speak or not and it is implicit in that choice he/she has accepted the risk of the contents of the conversation being made available to the police.

Ultimately the question of whether or not evidence obtained from a pretext conversation will be admitted is a question to be decided by the court. By following the procedures outlined in this Guide, admission of material into evidence cannot be guaranteed (as each case will depend on its own facts) but certainly a failure to comply will, more than likely, lead to exclusion.

Consideration Prior to Use

The following points should be carefully considered prior to conducting a pretext call.

Admissibility

There shall always be questions asked in relation to the legality and admissibility of pretext calls. The question of whether or not evidence obtained from a pretext call shall be admissible will be a question to be decided by the court.

• The recording of the pretext conversation must comply with the requirements of

Commonwealth and State legislation.

• It is essential to record the conversation with the witness and suspect only to

eliminate any suggestion police have been party to the conversation.

• If the police officer is present during the recorded conversation then there is a chance

the police will be deemed to be a third party and the recording determined as illegal.

Witness

The Investigating Officer (IO) must consider the nature of the offence, conduct a risk assessment of both the witness and the suspect prior to undertaking a pretext call. Particular considerations include:

• The age of the witness and their understanding of the nature of the call.

• Whether the witness is a victim.

• Whether the witness has any personal involvement with the suspect and the current

relationship between the witness and the suspect.

• When was the last time the witness was in contact with the suspect?

• Witness safety and security.

• The emotional state of the witness – consider the involvement of a counsellor or a

support person for the witness.

• How the phone number of the suspect was obtained – was a silent number used?

Given that Caller Identification applies to modern telephones make sure the caller uses a blocked phone line if using police premises. Alternatively, use the witness’s phone at home to overcome this problem.

Procedure

Witness

There are a number of issues the investigator should be mindful of when preparing a witness to make a pretext call:

• Ensure the witness has a clear understanding the content of the conversation is of

their own volition and police cannot tell them what to say.

• Advise the witness the purpose of the recording exercise. These instructions should

be limited to advising the witness of the purpose of the recording exercise. For example “Remember we are here today to concentrate on the sexual assault incident (or as the case may be), but what you say is entirely up to you”.

• Advise the witness on how to use the Digital Voice Recorder (DVR) and ensure the

ear piece does not touch the telephone.

• Ensure the witness is able to operate the equipment prior to calling the suspect.

• Provide the pretext conversation preamble to the witness and allow them to make the

call. See Addendum.

Equipment and Facilities

It is of the utmost importance that the equipment used to record the pretext call is set up and used strictly within the guidelines provided to ensure that all legal requirements are met and the evidence gathered is admissible in a court of law.

• The recording kit includes a DVR and an ear microphone that connects with the DVR.

Place the ear microphone in the caller’s ear so it is between the ear and the telephone receiver. You may use both hard-wired or landline phones and mobile phones when recording conversations this way. The microphone and receiver must not touch.

• Do not attach a recording device to the telephone (Telephone Intercept

requirements).

• To eliminate third party intervention and illegal monitoring of the recorded

conversation leave the caller in a private room on their own with the door closed. The caller is then allowed to operate the equipment and make the phone call in private.

• Ensure police or anyone else other than the two parties to the conversation do not

overhear, listen or monitor the conversation. There is no requirement for the caller to tell the suspect they are recording their conversation, as they are a party to the call.

(Refer section 5(3)(d) Surveillance Devices Act).

At the completion of the recorded conversation complete a formal statement containing the information from the preamble. A statement should be completed in every instance the caller has a conversation with the suspect whether admissions are made or not.

Any notes and conversations will be subject to disclosure and is to be recorded accordingly on the investigation file.

Suspect Interview

The interview with the suspect should be conducted utilising the current WA Police Force investigative interviewing guidelines. In regards to admissibility of the pretext call, and other evidence that may come from the interview, the following points must be taken into consideration:

• It is not recommended that you play the pretext call or refer to any admissions made

during the pretext call during the interview. There is a risk that, should you lose the pretext recording, any other admissions made in the interview may also be lost.

• It is preferable to ask the suspect about the receipt of the last call or contact with the

witness, and what they talked about rather than telling the suspect that you are aware of the pretext call.

• If the suspect denies contact with the witness, during interview, then the credibility of

the suspect will be diminished when the combined evidence is presented at trial.

Alternatively, if they admit the phone call took place, but offer a different version of the contents, then this will assist with establishing the credibility of the suspect and witness.

• Do not challenge the suspect during the interview on the content of the call or disclose

that police are aware or assisted in making the call. If the suspect speaks of the call of their own volition it is recommended the interviewer does not divulge knowledge of how or when the call was made.

Points of Note

Below are some points that should be stressed to the reader:

• The pretext should always be done prior to any suspect interview.

• The witness must be willing and agreeable to making the call with an assessment

made whether the call may further traumatise the witness.

• If a Violence Restraining Order is in place, a pretext is not an option.

• Modern oral electronic communication over a telephone does not always involve the

use of a telephone number. Social media applications (apps) allow people to speak to each other without the use of a telephone number. Consider and adapt to the possibility.

ADDENDUM – (Pretext telephone call instruction sheets)

Instructions: Sheet 1 (Police Officer)

The date is ______________________________, the time is _______________________.

This is a conversation between (investigating officer) ________________ of ____________________________________ concerning a pretext telephone call between:

________________________ (caller) and ______________________________ (recipient) by dialling telephone number or using the app, namely

_______________________________.

I cannot tell you what to say when you contact ________________________ today.

Remember, that you are here to concentrate on the matters referred to in your statement, and the complaint made by you.

You are in control of making the call so you can hang up at any time. While you’re making the call I cannot be involved in the conversation. To record the conversation we use this Olympus digital voice recorder model DS-2400 and ear microphone.

You need to place the large end of the ear microphone in your ear so that the conversation is recorded. The microphone on the ear piece will record what you say and what is being said to you. Remember to place the ear microphone in the same ear that you’ll place the telephone against.

It is important that the ear piece does not touch the telephone. The telephone number that you will be dialling or the app you will be using is _________________________________.

When the recording starts I will ask you to introduce yourself and explain what you are about to do by reading the following information [hand ‘Instructions: Sheet 2 (Witness)’ to the witness].

“I will now leave the room and allow you to make the call.”

Instructions: Sheet 2 (Witness)

Witness to read out once the recorder has been activated by the witness:

My full name is _________________________________________________.

Today is ______________________________________________________.

The time is _____________________.

I am currently at (address) ________________________________________________ and I am the person who activated this recording device. I activated this device freely and without any inducements or coercion from any person.

The telephone number/mobile app that I am about to use is _________________________.

I expect to speak with ______________________________________________________.

Dial the telephone number or use app, conduct the conversation, and then conclude conversation.

The person I spoke to was _________________________________________________.

The time is now ________________________________ and I will conclude this recording.

The recording may now be ceased.

Please return the device to the investigating officer.

Instructions: Sheet 3 (Police Officer)

Once the call is completed and recorder handed back to the police, the investigating officer is to play a portion of the tape to the witness for adoption purposes. Please complete the following:-

At _________________________________ am/pm on _____________________ the ________ of _____________________,

20____, at (address) ______________________________________________________ I received the above-mentioned digital voice recorder from ___________________________________________________________.

In his/her presence I rewound the device and played a portion of it.

(Witness) ______________________________________________ identified the voices on the device as himself/herself and___________________________________________- ___________.

I later transferred the recorded conversation onto a recordable compact disc and entered the recording onto the Frontline Incident Management System reference number - ____________________________________________________.

Instructions: Sheets 1 to 3 (inclusive) can later be described within the police witness statement and produced as ‘Physical Material’. It’s recommended that both the witness and police officer sign and date each sheet.
